question.p:
Question 1 of 304 : Physiology
At what concentration is the transport mechanism for glucose saturated? ... 300 mg/dL

Question 2 of 304 : Physiology
Define effective renal plasma flow. ... ERPF = U (PAH) x V/P (PAH) = C (PAH)

Question 3 of 304 : Physiology
Define filtration fraction. ... FF = GFR/ RPF

Question 4 of 304 : Physiology
Define free water clearance. ... C(H2O) = V- C(osm)

Question 5 of 304 : Physiology
Define GFR. ... GFR = U(inulin) x V/P (inulin) = C (inulin) GFR also equals the difference in (osmotic pressure of the glomerular capillary minus Bowman's space) and (hydrostatic pressure of the glomerular capsule minus Bowman's space).

Question 6 of 304 : Physiology
Define renal blood flow. ... RBF = RPF/1 - Hct

Question 7 of 304 : Physiology
Define renal clearance. ... Cx = UxV/Px The volume of plasma from which the substance is cleared completely per unit time.

Question 8 of 304 : Physiology
Define urine flow rate. ... V = urine flow rate C (osm) = U(osm)V/P(osm)

Question 9 of 304 : Physiology
How are amino acids cleared in the kidney? ... Reabsorption occurs by at least 3 distinct carrier systems, with competitive inhibition within each group.

Question 10 of 304 : Physiology
How do NSAIDs cause renal failure? ... By inhibiting the production of prostaglandins which normally keep the afferent arterioles vasodilated to maintain GFR

Question 11 of 304 : Physiology
How high can the osmolarity of the medulla reach? ... 1200-1400 mOsm

Question 12 of 304 : Physiology
How is ICF measured? ... ICF = TBW - ECF

Question 13 of 304 : Physiology
How is interstitial volume measured? ... Interstitial volume = ECF - PV

Question 14 of 304 : Physiology
How is PAH secreted? ... Via secondary active transport

Question 15 of 304 : Physiology
How is PAH transport mediated? ... Mediated by a carrier system for organic acids

Question 16 of 304 : Physiology
How much of the ECF is interstitial fluid? ... Three-fourths

Question 17 of 304 : Physiology
How much of the ECF is plasma? ... One-fourth

Question 18 of 304 : Physiology
How much of the total body water is part of intracellular fluid? ... Two-thirds

Question 19 of 304 : Physiology
How much of the total body water is part of the extracellular fluid? ... One-third

Question 20 of 304 : Physiology
If clearance of substance X is equal to GFR, what occurs? ... There is no net secretion or reabsorption

Question 21 of 304 : Physiology
If clearance of substance X is greater than GFR, what occurs? ... Net tubular secretion of X

Question 22 of 304 : Physiology
If clearance of substance X is less than GFR, what occurs? ... Net tubular reabsorption of X

Question 23 of 304 : Physiology
T/F. Secondary active transport of amino acids is saturable. ... TRUE

Question 24 of 304 : Physiology
What 3 layers form the glomerular filtration barrier? ... 1. Fenestrated capillary endothelium 2. Fused basement membrane with heparan sulfate 3. Epithelial layer consisting of podocyte foot processes

Question 25 of 304 : Physiology
What actions does ADH have on the kidney? ... -Increase water permeability of principle cells in collecting ducts -Increase urea absorption in CD -Increase Na/K/2Cl transporter in the thick ascending limb

Question 26 of 304 : Physiology
What actions does AII have on the kidney? ... -Contraction of efferent arteriole increasing GFR -Increased Na and HCO3 reabsorption in proximal tubule

Question 27 of 304 : Physiology
What actions does aldo have on the kidneys? ... -Increased Na reabsorption in distal tubule -Increased K secretion in DT -Increased H ion secretion in DT

Question 28 of 304 : Physiology
What actions does ANP have on the kidney? ... -Decreased Na reabsorption -Increased GFR

Question 29 of 304 : Physiology
What actions does PTH have on the kideny? ... -Increased Ca reabsorption -Decreased phosphate reabsorption -Increase 1,25-(OH)2 Vit D production

Question 30 of 304 : Physiology
What activates 1 alpha-hydroxylase? ... PTH

Question 31 of 304 : Physiology
What are the 4 actions of angiotensin II? ... 1. Vasoconstriction 2. Release of aldo from adrenal cortex 3. Release of ADH from posterior pituitary 4. Stimulates hypothalamus to increase thirst

Question 32 of 304 : Physiology
What are the 4 endocrine functions of the kidney? ... 1. EPO release 2. Vitamin D conversion 3. Renin release 4. Prostaglandins release

Question 33 of 304 : Physiology
What are the consequences of a loss in the charge barrier? ... -Albuminuria -Hypoproteinemia -Generalized edema -Hyperlipidemia

Question 34 of 304 : Physiology
What competitively inhibits the carrier system for PAH? ... Probenecid

Question 35 of 304 : Physiology
What constricts the efferent arteriole? ... Angiotensin II

Question 36 of 304 : Physiology
What dilates the renal afferent arteriole? ... Prostaglandins

Question 37 of 304 : Physiology
What do the collecting ducts reabsorb in exchange for K or H? ... Na ions

Question 38 of 304 : Physiology
What does renin do? ... Cleave angiotensinogen into angiotensin I

Question 39 of 304 : Physiology
What does the anterior pituitary secrete? ... -FSH and LH -ACTH -GH -TSH -MSH -Prolactin

Question 40 of 304 : Physiology
What does the beta subunit do? ... The beta subunit determines hormone specificity

Question 41 of 304 : Physiology
What does the early distal convoluted tubule actively reabsorb? ... -Na ions -Cl ions

Question 42 of 304 : Physiology
What does the posterior pituitary secrete? ... ADH and oxytocin

Question 43 of 304 : Physiology
What does the secretion of prostaglandins from the kidney do? ... Vasodilates the afferent arterioles to increase GFR

Question 44 of 304 : Physiology
What does the thick ascending loop of Henle actively reabsorb? ... -Na ions -K ions -Cl ions

Question 45 of 304 : Physiology
What does the thick descending loop of Henle indirectly reabsorb? ... -Mg ion -Ca ions

Question 46 of 304 : Physiology
What effect does constriction of the efferent arteriole have? ... -Decreased RPF -Increased GFR -FF increases

Question 47 of 304 : Physiology
What effect does dilation of the afferent arteriole have? ... -Increased RPF -Increased GFR - FF remains constant

Question 48 of 304 : Physiology
What enzyme converts 25-OH Vit D to 1,25-(OH)2 Vit D? ... 1alpha-hydroxylase

Question 49 of 304 : Physiology
What happens to glucose in the kidneys when glucose is at a normal level? ... Glucose is completely reabsorbed in the proximal tubule.

Question 50 of 304 : Physiology
What hormones act on the kidney? ... 1. ADH 2. Aldosterone 3. Angiotensin II 4. Atrial natriurtic Peptide 5. PTH

Question 51 of 304 : Physiology
What inhibits constriction of the efferent arteriole by AII? ... ACE inhibitors

Question 52 of 304 : Physiology
What inhibits dilation of the afferent arteriole by prostaglandins? ... NSAIDS

Question 53 of 304 : Physiology
What is an important clinical clue to diabetes? ... Glucosuria

Question 54 of 304 : Physiology
What is angiotensin II's overall function? ... To increase intravascular volume and increase blood pressure

Question 55 of 304 : Physiology
What is passively reabsorbed in the thin descending loop of Henle? ... Water via medullary hypertonicity (impermeable to sodium)

Question 56 of 304 : Physiology
What is reabsorbed in the early distal tubule under the control of PTH? ... Ca ions

Question 57 of 304 : Physiology
What is the function of the early proximal convoluted tubule? ... Reabsorbs all of the glucose and amino acids and most of the bicarbonate, sodium, and water

Question 58 of 304 : Physiology
What is the oncotic pressure of Bowman's space? ... Zero

Question 59 of 304 : Physiology
What is the thick ascending loop of Henle impermeable to? ... Water

Question 60 of 304 : Physiology
What is the threshold for glucose reabsorption in the proximal tubule? ... 200 mg/dL

Question 61 of 304 : Physiology
What may act as a 'check' on the renin-angiotensin system in heart failure? ... ANP

Question 62 of 304 : Physiology
What part of the nephron secretes ammonia? ... Early proximal convoluted tubule

Question 63 of 304 : Physiology
What part of the pituitary is derived from neuroectoderm? ... Posterior pituitary

Question 64 of 304 : Physiology
What percentage of the body is water? ... 0.6

Question 65 of 304 : Physiology
What regulates the reabsorption of water in the collecting ducts? ... ADH

Question 66 of 304 : Physiology
What secretes renin? ... JG cells

Question 67 of 304 : Physiology
What stimulates ADH secretion? ... -Increased plasma osmolarity -Greatly decreased blood volume

Question 68 of 304 : Physiology
What stimulates aldosterone secretion? ... -Decreased blood volume (via AII) -Increased plasma K concentration

Question 69 of 304 : Physiology
What stimulates angiotensin secretion? ... Decreased blood volume (via renin)

Question 70 of 304 : Physiology
What stimulates ANP secretion? ... Increased atrial pressure

Question 71 of 304 : Physiology
What stimulates EPO release? ... Hypoxia

Question 72 of 304 : Physiology
What stimulates PTH secretion? ... Decreased plasma ca concentration

Question 73 of 304 : Physiology
What stimulates renin release? ... 1. Decreased renal arterial pressure 2. Increased renal nerve discharge (Beta 1 effect)

Question 74 of 304 : Physiology
What subunit do TSH, LH, FSH and hCG have in common? ... Alpha subunit

Question 75 of 304 : Physiology
What symptom is present once threshold is reached? ... Glucosuria

Question 76 of 304 : Physiology
What type of tissue is the anterior pituitary derived from? ... Oral ectoderm

Question 77 of 304 : Physiology
What value is used clinically to represent GFR? ... Creatinine clearance

Question 78 of 304 : Physiology
What variables are needed to calculate free water clearance? ... -Urine flow rate -Urine osmolarity -Plasma Osmolarity

Question 79 of 304 : Physiology
Where does ACE convert AI to AII? ... Primarily the lung capillaries

Question 80 of 304 : Physiology
Where does secondary active transport of amino acids occur? ... In the proximal tubule

Question 81 of 304 : Physiology
Where is EPO secreted? ... Endothelial cells of the peritubular capillaries (kidney)

Question 82 of 304 : Physiology
Where is paraaminohippuric acid secreted? ... Proximal tubule

Question 83 of 304 : Physiology
Which barrier is lost in nephrotic syndrome? ... Charge barrier

Question 84 of 304 : Physiology
Which layer filters by negative charge? ... Fused basement membrane

Question 85 of 304 : Physiology
Which layer filters by size? ... Fenestrated capillary endothelium

Question 86 of 304 : Physiology
Why does the nephron secrete ammonia? ... Acts As a buffer for secreted H ions

Question 87 of 304 : Physiology
Why is inulin used to measure GFR? ... Because it is freely filtered and is neither absorbed or secreted

Question 88 of 304 : Physiology
Why is PAH used to calculate RPF? ... PAH is secreted and filtered.

Question 89 of 304 : Physiology
A 21-Beta-hydroxylase deficiency will result in what hormone deficiencies/excesses? ... Decreased cortisol and mineralocorticoids (hypotension, hyperkalemia) Increased sex hormones (masculinization)

Question 90 of 304 : Physiology
A deficiency of 17-alpha hydroxylase will result in an decrease in what hormone(s)? ... Decreased sex hormones and cortisol

Question 91 of 304 : Physiology
A deficiency of 17-alpha hydroxylase will result in an increase in what hormone(s)? ... Aldosterone Produces hypertension, hypokalemia

Question 92 of 304 : Physiology
A dopaminergic antagonist would be expected to have what effect on prolactin secretion? ... Stimulates prolactin secretion.

Question 93 of 304 : Physiology
A maturing graafian follicule can be found at what stage of the menstrual cycle? ... During the proliferative phase (Around Day 7)

Question 94 of 304 : Physiology
Angiotensin II has what effect on the adrenal cortex? ... Stimulates aldosterone production by enhancing the activity of aldosterone synthase

Question 95 of 304 : Physiology
Calcitonin's actions (synergize/oppose) the actions of PTH? ... Oppose. Calcitonin acts faster than PTH to decrease serum Ca2+ levels.

Question 96 of 304 : Physiology
Decreased cortisol levels as in any of the congenital adrenal hyperplasias will have what effect on ACTH? ... ACTH levels will be increased contributing to increased skin pigmentation

Question 97 of 304 : Physiology
Decreased phosphate will have what effect on Vit D? ... Increased activated Vit D.

Question 98 of 304 : Physiology
During the 2nd and 3rd trimester, one would expect the corpus luteum to be? ... Degenerated. Shortly after the first trimester, the placenta makes estriol and progesterone.

Question 99 of 304 : Physiology
Estradiol is converted from what precursor by what enzyme? ... Aromatase converts Testosterone to Estradiol.

Question 100 of 304 : Physiology
Estrogen is produced in what 4 locations in the body? ... Corpus luteum, placenta, adrenal cortex, and testes

Question 101 of 304 : Physiology
Estrogen levels are low/med/high just before the peak of the LH surge? ... High. Estrogen switches to positive feedback of LH from negative so both increase.

Question 102 of 304 : Physiology
Estrogens have what effect of LH secretion? ... Complex effects. Early on estrogen has a negative effect that switches to positve just before the LH surge.

Question 103 of 304 : Physiology
Estrogens have what effect of the follicle? ... Estrogens stimulate growth of the follicle

Question 104 of 304 : Physiology
Failure of brain maturation due to lack of thyroid hormone is known as? ... Cretinism

Question 105 of 304 : Physiology
Finasteride inhibits what step in testosterone metabolism? ... Converstion of testosterone to DHT by 5-alpha reductase

Question 106 of 304 : Physiology
Follicular growth is fastest during what part of the menstrual cycle? ... During the second week of the proliferative phase (Days 7-14)

Question 107 of 304 : Physiology
FSH stimulates what cells in the male? ... Sertoli cells (spermatogenesis)

Question 108 of 304 : Physiology
Hypocalcemia will have what effect on Vit D metabolism? ... Decreased Ca2+ will increase PTH which will stimulate the kidney to produce more activated Vit D.

Question 109 of 304 : Physiology
In addition to peripheral conversion, DHT is also produced in the? ... Prostate

Question 110 of 304 : Physiology
In what organ is Vitamin D3 produced? ... The skin. Vit D requires sun exposure (UV light and heat)

Question 111 of 304 : Physiology
Is testosterone considered to be anabolic or catabolic overall? ... Anabolic

Question 112 of 304 : Physiology
LH levels would be low/med/high at the time of ovulation (Day 14) ... Low. The LH surge has already declined

Question 113 of 304 : Physiology
LH stimulates what cells in the male? ... Leydig cells (testosterone synthesis)

Question 114 of 304 : Physiology
Name the two primary insulin independent organs? ... Brain and RBC's take up glucose independent of insulin

Question 115 of 304 : Physiology
Order the following with the most potent first: testosterone, androstenedione. DHT ... DHT > testosterone > androstenedione

Question 116 of 304 : Physiology
Order the following with the most potent first: estrone, estradiol, estriol. ... Estradiol > estrone > estriol

Question 117 of 304 : Physiology
Phosphate reabsortion in the kidneys is inhibited by what hormone? ... PTH

Question 118 of 304 : Physiology
Progesterone has what effect on body temperature? ... Increases body temperature

Question 119 of 304 : Physiology
Progestorone is used in combination with estrogen for what reason? ... To decrease the risk of endometrial cancer associated with unopposed estrogen therapy

Question 120 of 304 : Physiology
Prolactin has what effect on ovulation? ... Prolactin inhibits ovulation by inhibiting the release/synthess of GnRH from the hypothalamus

Question 121 of 304 : Physiology
PTH causes increased calcium reabsorption in what part of the kidney? ... DCT

Question 122 of 304 : Physiology
PTH is produced by what cell type? ... Chief cells of the parathyroid glands

Question 123 of 304 : Physiology
Sertoli cells stimulate spermatogenesis by producing what 2 factors in response to FSH? ... Androgen-binding protein (ABP) - concentrates testosterone in the seminiferous tubules Inhibin - inhibits FSH secretion fro the ant pit

Question 124 of 304 : Physiology
T/F - Glycolisis is promoted by the thyroid hormones? ... False. Thyroid hormones increase blood glucose levels by stimulating glycolgenolysis and gluconeogenesis.

Question 125 of 304 : Physiology
T/F - PTH stimulates both osteoclasts and osteoblasts? ... True

Question 126 of 304 : Physiology
T/F - Testosterone is the most active androgen in males and females? ... False. 5-alpha reductase activates testosterone to DHT which is the most active androgen.

Question 127 of 304 : Physiology
Testosterone acts as a negative inhibitor on what hormone from the brain? ... GnRH

Question 128 of 304 : Physiology
Testosterone is synthesized in what two locations? ... Testis Adrenal Cortex

Question 129 of 304 : Physiology
The hormone with the highest concentration during the secretory phase is? ... Progesterone

Question 130 of 304 : Physiology
The key inhibitor of prolactin release is? ... Dopamine secreted from the hypothalamus Bromocriptine (Dopamine agonist has the same effect)

Question 131 of 304 : Physiology
The parathyroid glands come from what embryonic structures? ... The 3rd and 4th pharyngeal pouches

Question 132 of 304 : Physiology
The primary estrogen produced by the ovary is? ... Estradiol

Question 133 of 304 : Physiology
The primary estrogen produced by the placenta is? ... Estriol

Question 134 of 304 : Physiology
Thick mucous production is the result of what sex hormone? ... Progesterone Decreases sperm entry into the uterus

Question 135 of 304 : Physiology
Thyroid Stimulating Immunoglobulin results in what disease? ... Graves Disease (hyperthyroidism)

Question 136 of 304 : Physiology
Thyroid hormones acts synergistically with what hormone with respect to bone growth? ... GH

Question 137 of 304 : Physiology
TRH is produced in what region of the brain? ... Hypothalamus

Question 138 of 304 : Physiology
TSH levels in a hypothyroid patient would be? Free T4? ... Elevated TSH, Decreased free T4.

Question 139 of 304 : Physiology
Unlike estrogen, what effect does progesterone have on the myometrium? ... Progesterone decreases myometrial excitability to help maintain the pregnancy/facilitate fertilization

Question 140 of 304 : Physiology
Vit D deficiency in kids cause what disease? Adults? ... Rickets in kids Osteomalacia in adults

Question 141 of 304 : Physiology
What 2 conditions other than pregnancy increase hCG? ... Hydatidiform moles in women or choriocarcinoma

Question 142 of 304 : Physiology
What adrenergic effects do the thyroid hormones have? ... Beta-adrenergic effects

Question 143 of 304 : Physiology
What are the symptoms of menopause? ... HAVOC H = Hot flashes A V = Atrophy of the Vagina O = Osteoporosis C = Coronary Artery Disease

Question 144 of 304 : Physiology
What cells produce calcitonin? ... Parafollicular cells (C cells) of the thyroid

Question 145 of 304 : Physiology
What does an elevated progesterone level indicate? ... Ovulation

Question 146 of 304 : Physiology
What effect do androgens have on growth of long bones ... During puberty, testosterone stimulates bone growth but eventually causes closure of the epiphyseal plates

Question 147 of 304 : Physiology
What effect do estrogens have on the endometrium? Myometrium? ... Stimulate endometrial proliferation Increase myometrial excitability

Question 148 of 304 : Physiology
What effect do estrogens have on the liver? ... Increase hepatic synthesis of transport proteins

Question 149 of 304 : Physiology
What effect do the thyroid hormones have on cardiac output? Heart rate? Contractility? Stroke Volume? Respiratory Rate? ... Thyroid hormones increase: CO HR SV contractility and RR

Question 150 of 304 : Physiology
What effect does PTH have on bone? ... Stimulates bone resorption of calcium.

Question 151 of 304 : Physiology
What effect does progesterone have on FSH? On LH? ... Progesterone is inhibitory to both gonadotrophins

Question 152 of 304 : Physiology
What effect does progesterone have on the endometrium? ... Progesterone stimulates the endometrial glands to become secretory and increases spiral artery development

Question 153 of 304 : Physiology
What effect does PTH have on bone? ... Increases bone resorption of Ca2+ and phosphate

Question 154 of 304 : Physiology
What effect does thyroid hormone have on lipolysis? ... Lipolysis is stimulated

Question 155 of 304 : Physiology
What effect will low serum phosphate have the kidney? ... The kidney will produce more 1-25-OH2 Vit D which will increase phosphate release from bone matrix and increase Ca2+ and phosphate absorption in the GIT

Question 156 of 304 : Physiology
What enzyme deficiency will produce BOTH hypertension and masculinization of females? ... 11-Beta hydroxylase deficiency 11-deoxycorticosterone will act as a mineralocorticoid

Question 157 of 304 : Physiology
What enzyme in the kidney is stimulated that affects vitamin D metabolism? ... PTH stimulates 1-alpha-hydroxylase cause increased production of 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D.

Question 158 of 304 : Physiology
What happens to the corpus lutem if progesterone levels fall without fertilization? ... The corpus luteum regresses and menstration occurs

Question 159 of 304 : Physiology
What happens to the corpus lutem and progesterone levels with fertilization? ... The corpus luteum is maintained by hCG acting like LH which maintains both estrogen and progesterone levels.

Question 160 of 304 : Physiology
What hormonal changes are seen with untreated menopause with respect to estrogen, FSH, LH, GnRH? ... Decreased estrogen Increased FSH (Greatly) Increased LH (No surge) Increased GnRH

Question 161 of 304 : Physiology
What hormone predominates during the secretory phase of the menstrual cycle? ... Progesterone

Question 162 of 304 : Physiology
What is the key regulator of PTH secretion? ... Decrease in free serum Ca2+ increases PTH secretion. Increased Ca2+ feedback inhibits PTH secretion.

Question 163 of 304 : Physiology
What is the key regulator that increases Calcitonin secretion? ... Increased serum Ca2+

Question 164 of 304 : Physiology
What is the most common cause of congenital adrenal hyperplasia? ... 21-Beta hydroxylase deficiency

Question 165 of 304 : Physiology
What is the physiologic source of hCG? ... The syncytiotrophoblasts of the placenta

Question 166 of 304 : Physiology
What is the primary organ that converts Vit D to 25-OH Vit D? ... Liver

Question 167 of 304 : Physiology
What is the primary source of androstenedione? ... Adrenal glands

Question 168 of 304 : Physiology
What is the role of calcitonin in normal calcium homeostasis? ... Probably not important as PTH which is the primary regulator of calcium homeostasis.

Question 169 of 304 : Physiology
What is the VERY first molecule in the pathway for the synthesis of Aldosterone? Cortisol? Adrenal androgens? ... Cholesterol

Question 170 of 304 : Physiology
What is thought to be the cause of menopause? ... Cessation of estrogen production due to decline in the number of follicles

Question 171 of 304 : Physiology
What overall effects does PTH have on body electolytes? ... PTH increases serum Ca 2+, decreases serum phosphates, increases urine phosphates

Question 172 of 304 : Physiology
What signal from the body decreases TRH secretion? ... Thyroid hormones, T3

Question 173 of 304 : Physiology
What substance is used by the brain for energy during starvation? ... Ketone bodies

Question 174 of 304 : Physiology
What will the levels of Ca2+, phosphate, and alkaline phosphatase be in hyperparathyroidism? ... Increased Ca2+, decreased phosphate, increased alkaline phosphatase

Question 175 of 304 : Physiology
What will the levels of Ca2+, phosphate, and alkaline phosphatase be in osteoporosis? ... No changes in Ca2+, phosphate, or alkaline phosphatase

Question 176 of 304 : Physiology
What will the levels of Ca2+, phosphate, and alkaline phosphatase be in Paget's disease of bone? ... Alkaline phosphatase increased with normal Ca2+ and phosphate

Question 177 of 304 : Physiology
What will the levels of Ca2+, phosphate, and alkaline phosphatase be in renal insufficiency? ... Decreased Ca2+, increased phosphate, and alkaline phosphates WNL

Question 178 of 304 : Physiology
What will the levels of Ca2+, phosphate, and alkaline phosphatase be in Vit D intoxication? ... Increased Ca2+ and phosphate with alkaline phosphatase WNL

Question 179 of 304 : Physiology
Which ducts (Mullerian or Wolfian) are promoted by androgens? ... Wolfian ducts are differentiated into the internal gonadal structures.

Question 180 of 304 : Physiology
Why is hCG so useful for detecting pregnancy? ... It is detectable in the blood and urine 8 days after successful fertilization.

Question 181 of 304 : Physiology
Why is hormone replacement therapy used in postmenopausal women? ... Decrease hot flashes and decrease bone loss. Decreased risk of heart disease could be on the boards but is no longer true (2001).

Question 182 of 304 : Physiology
Will most steroids in the blood be bound or unbound? ... Bound to specific binding globulins Steroids are lipophilic

Question 183 of 304 : Physiology
You would expect the body temperature of a patient with hyperthroidism to be? ... Elevated Thyroid hormone increases Na/K ATPase activity => increased consumption of O2 => increased temp

Question 184 of 304 : Physiology
A decrease in PA O2 will have what effect on the pulmonary vasculature? ... Causes hypoxic vasoconstriction that shifts blood awayfrom poorly ventilated regions

Question 185 of 304 : Physiology
A value of infinity for V/Q indicates? ... Blood flow obstruction

Question 186 of 304 : Physiology
A ZERO value for V/Q indicates? ... Airway obstruction

Question 187 of 304 : Physiology
Bicarbonate in the RBC is transported out of the cell in exchange for what ion? ... Cl- by a HCO3-/Cl- antiport

Question 188 of 304 : Physiology
Cor pulmonale is the result of? ... Pulmonary hypertension

Question 189 of 304 : Physiology
Cor pulmonale will lead to what condition of the heart? ... Right ventricular failure (jugular venous distention, edema, hepatomegaly)

Question 190 of 304 : Physiology
Dissociation of CO2 from Hb upon oxygenation in the lungs is known as? ... The Haldane effect

Question 191 of 304 : Physiology
Exercise (increased cardiac output) will have what effect on V/Q to the apex? ... The V/Q will approach 1 (from 3) as a result of dilation of vessels in the apex.

Question 192 of 304 : Physiology
In the apex of the lung, V/Q should be >1, =1, or <1? ... V/Q > 1. NL = 3 which indicates wasted ventilation.

Question 193 of 304 : Physiology
In the base of the lung, V/Q should be >1, =1, or <1? ... V/Q < 1. NL = 0.6 which indicates wasted perfusion.

Question 194 of 304 : Physiology
In the perpheral tissue what factor helps unload oxygen by shifting the curve to the right? ... Increased H+ (decreased pH) a.k.a. the Bohr effect

Question 195 of 304 : Physiology
Increased 2,3-DPG will cause a shift in what direction of the oxygen-Hb dissociation curve? ... The curve will shift RIGHT. This allows Hb to release more oxygen

Question 196 of 304 : Physiology
Increased erythropoietin levels as a response to high altitudes will have what affect on the blood? ... Increase hematocrit and Hb

Question 197 of 304 : Physiology
Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome is due to a deficiency of what? ... Surfactant (dipalmitoyl phosphatidylcholine, lecithin)

Question 198 of 304 : Physiology
Perfusion is greatest in what part of the lung? ... Both ventilation and perfusion are greater at the base than at the apex.

Question 199 of 304 : Physiology
Recurrent TB grows best in what part of the lung? Why? ... Apex because of high O2.

Question 200 of 304 : Physiology
Surfactant role in the lungs is to do what? ... Decrease alveolar surface tension

Question 201 of 304 : Physiology
T/F - The pulmorary circulation is a high resistance, low compliance system. ... F. It has low resistance and high compliance.

Question 202 of 304 : Physiology
The conversion of CO2 to H2CO3 (Carbonic acid) is catalyzed by what RBC enzyme? ... Carbonic Anhydrase

Question 203 of 304 : Physiology
The kidneys would do what to compensate for respiratory alkalosis as a response to high altitude? ... Excrete bicarbonate

Question 204 of 304 : Physiology
The predominant form of CO2 transport from the tissues to the lungs is? ... HCO3- (bicarbonate) accounts for 90%, followed by Hb bound CO2 (5%) and dissolved CO2 (5%)

Question 205 of 304 : Physiology
TV+IRV+ERV = ? TV = tidal volume, IRV = inspirartory reserve volume, ERV = expiratory reserve volume ... Vital capacity. VC is everything but the residual volume.

Question 206 of 304 : Physiology
Ventilation is greatest in what part of the lung? ... Both ventilation and perfusion are greater at the base than at the apex.

Question 207 of 304 : Physiology
What 6 factors decrease O2 affinity to Hb/decrease P50? What direction does the O2-Hb dissociation curve shift? ... Decrease metabolic needs, dcr PCO2, dcr temperature, increased pH, dcr 2,3-DPG, and Fetal Hb The curve shifts LEFT.

Question 208 of 304 : Physiology
What are some potential side effects of ACE inhibitors? ... Cough and angioedema due to decreased bradykinin

Question 209 of 304 : Physiology
What cellular change could you expect as a response to high altitude? ... Increased mitochondria

Question 210 of 304 : Physiology
What enzyme in the lungs is a key enzyme in the renin-angiotensin system? ... Angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) which converts Ang I to Ang II

Question 211 of 304 : Physiology
What is expiratory reserve volume? ... Air that can still be breathed out after normal expiration

Question 212 of 304 : Physiology
What is FRC? How is it calculated? ... FRC is the flume in the lungs after normal respiration and is the sum of RV +ERV.

Question 213 of 304 : Physiology
What is inspiratory reserve volume? ... Air in excess of the tidal volume that moves into the lungs with maximum inspiration

Question 214 of 304 : Physiology
What is residual volume? ... Air in the lung at maximal expiration

Question 215 of 304 : Physiology
What is the bodies acute reponse to a change from low to high altitude? ... Increase in ventilation

Question 216 of 304 : Physiology
What is the difference between capacites and volumes in the lung? ... Capacities are the sum of >= 2 volumes.

Question 217 of 304 : Physiology
What is the Total Lung Capacity? Normal Value? ... IRV + TV + ERV + RV or VC + RV Normal would be ~ 6.0 L

Question 218 of 304 : Physiology
What is tidal volume? What is a normal TV value? ... Air that moves into the lung with each quiet expiration. 500 mL is normal

Question 219 of 304 : Physiology
What would be the effect on the heart due to chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (High altitude)? ... Right ventricular hypertrophy

Question 220 of 304 : Physiology
Would you expect acidosis or alkalosis due as a response to high altitude? Metabolic or Respiratory? ... Respiratory alakalosis

Question 221 of 304 : Physiology
Exocrine secretion of zymogens by secretory acini is stimulated by what? ... -Acetylcholine -CCK

Question 222 of 304 : Physiology
effects of Parasympathetic GI Innervation: ... 1. Increase production of saliva 2. Increase gastric H+ secretion 3. Increases pancreatic enzyme and HCO3- secretion 4. Stimulates evteric nervous system to creat intestinal peristalsis 5. Relaxes sphincters

Question 223 of 304 : Physiology
Five main components of gastric secretions and their sources? ... -Mucus (Mucous cell) -Intrinsic factor (Parietal cell) -H+ (Parietal cell) -Pepsinogen (Chief cell) -Gastrin (G cell in antrum and duodenum)

Question 224 of 304 : Physiology
Four categories of drugs that inhibit/decrease secretion of gastric acid: ... 1. Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole) 2. H2 receptor antagonists (Rantidine, Cimetidine, Famotidine) 3. Anticholinergics 4. Prostaglandin receptor antagonists (Misoprostol)

Question 225 of 304 : Physiology
Four effects of Sympathetic GI Innervation: ... 1. Increase production of saliva 2. Decreases splanchnic blood flow in fight-or-flight response 3. Decreases motility 4. Constricts Sphincters

Question 226 of 304 : Physiology
Four functions of H+ secreted in the stomach? ... -Kills bacteria -Breaks down food -Lowers pH to optimal range for pepsin function (conversion of pepsinoget) -Sterilizes chyme

Question 227 of 304 : Physiology
Four functions of Samatostatin? ... 1. Inhibits Gastric acid and pepsinogen secretion 2. Inhibits pancreatic and small intestine fluid secretion 3. Gallbladder contraction 4. Release of both insulin and glucagon

Question 228 of 304 : Physiology
From what cells is bile secreted? ... hepatocytes

Question 229 of 304 : Physiology
Function of Gastrin secreted in the stomach? ... Stimulates secretion of HCl, IF, and pepsinogen (also stimulates gastric motility)

Question 230 of 304 : Physiology
Function of Intrinsic factor secreted in the stomach? ... Binding protein required for vitamin B12 absorption (in terminal ileum)

Question 231 of 304 : Physiology
How do you treat Pancreatic Insufficiency? ... -Limit fat intake -Monitor for signs of fat-soluble vitamin (A,D,E,K) deficiency

Question 232 of 304 : Physiology
How does jaundice manifest in the body? ... yellow skin and sclerae

Question 233 of 304 : Physiology
How much urobilinogen is secreted per day? ... 4mg

Question 234 of 304 : Physiology
In what form is bilirubin secreted by the kidney? ... urobilirubin

Question 235 of 304 : Physiology
In what form is bilirubin secreted in the feces? ... stercobilin

Question 236 of 304 : Physiology
Name as many Pancreatic enzymes as you can: ... -alpha-amylase -lipase -phospholipase A -colipase -proteases (trypsin, chymotrypsin, elastase, carboxypeptidases) -trypsinogen (trypsin)

Question 237 of 304 : Physiology
Name the major product of heme metabolism that is actively taken up by hepatocytes: ... Bilirubin

Question 238 of 304 : Physiology
the organ and enzyme family involved in the production of bilirubin? ... Nonerythroid enzymes in the liver

Question 239 of 304 : Physiology
Name the three salivary secretory glands: ... -Porotid -Submandibular -Sublingual

Question 240 of 304 : Physiology
Name two potent stimulators of Gastrin: ... 1. Phenylalanine 2. Tryptophan

Question 241 of 304 : Physiology
Secretin's nickname? ... Nature's antacid

Question 242 of 304 : Physiology
Three main functions of CCK? ... 1. Stimulates gallbladder contraction 2. Stimulates pancreatic enzyme secretion 3. Inhibits gastric emptying

Question 243 of 304 : Physiology
Two functions of Secretin? ... 1. Stimulates pancreatic HCO3 secretion 2. Inhibits gastric acid secretion

Question 244 of 304 : Physiology
Two functions of the mucus secreted in the stomach? ... -Lubricant -protects surface from H+

Question 245 of 304 : Physiology
What activates all the proteases? ... trypsin

Question 246 of 304 : Physiology
What are the products of oligosaccharide hydrolase action? ... Monosaccharides (glucose, galactose, fructose)

Question 247 of 304 : Physiology
What are the products of starch hydrolysis by pancreatic amylase? ... Oligosaccharides, maltose and maltotriose

Question 248 of 304 : Physiology
What are the products of the hydrolysis of carbohydrate alpha-1,4 linkages by salivary amylase? ... maltose, maltotriose and alpha-limit dextrans

Question 249 of 304 : Physiology
What are the three main functions of saliva? ... 1. Begin starch digestion 2. Neutralize oral bacterial acids which maintains dental health 3. Lubricate food

Question 250 of 304 : Physiology
What are the two main sources of bilirubin in the body? ... -Hepatic production by nonerythroid enzymes -Metabolism of heme from red blood cells (120 day life span) and incomplete or immature erythroid cells

Question 251 of 304 : Physiology
What causes pain to worsen in Cholelithiasis? ... Eating fatty foods which cause CCK release

Question 252 of 304 : Physiology
What component of GI secretion is 'not essetial for digestion?' ... Gastric acid

Question 253 of 304 : Physiology
What condition results from elevated bilirubin levels? ... Jaundice

Question 254 of 304 : Physiology
What disease is commonly associated with pancreatic insufficiency? ... Cystic Fibrosis

Question 255 of 304 : Physiology
What do pancreatic ducts secrete when stimulated by secretin? ... -mucus -alkaline fluid

Question 256 of 304 : Physiology
What does inadequate gastric acid cause? ... Increased risk of Salmonella infections

Question 257 of 304 : Physiology
What enzyme converts trypsinogen to trypsin? ... enterokinase (a duodenal brushborder enzyme)

Question 258 of 304 : Physiology
What enzyme hydrolyzes starch? ... Pancreatic amylase

Question 259 of 304 : Physiology
What enzyme is involved in the rate-limiting step in carbohydrate digestion? ... Oligosaccaride hydrolases

Question 260 of 304 : Physiology
What enzyme starts digestion and hydrolyzes alpha-1,4 linkages? ... Salivary Amylase

Question 261 of 304 : Physiology
What form are the proteases secreted in? ... proenzyme form

Question 262 of 304 : Physiology
What form is Alpha-amylase secreted in? ... active form

Question 263 of 304 : Physiology
What hormone decreases absorption of substances needed for growth? ... Somatostatin

Question 264 of 304 : Physiology
What inhibits the release of gastrin and secretin? ... Somatostatin

Question 265 of 304 : Physiology
Where is pancreatic amylase in highest concentration? ... In the duodenal lumen

Question 266 of 304 : Physiology
What is the composition of bile? (5) ... -bile salts -phospholipids -cholesterol -bilirubin -water

Question 267 of 304 : Physiology
What is the fate of pepsinogen? ... Broken down to pepsin (a protease) by H+

Question 268 of 304 : Physiology
What is the function (fxn) of Pepsin? ... Begins protein digestion (optimal pH = 1.0 - 3.0)

Question 269 of 304 : Physiology
What is the function of Alpha-amylase? ... starch digestion

Question 270 of 304 : Physiology
What is the function of proteases? ... protein digestion

Question 271 of 304 : Physiology
What is the function of VIP? ... -pancreatic HCO3- secretion - intibition of gastric H+ secretion

Question 272 of 304 : Physiology
What is the function on Nitrous Oxide? ... Causes smooth muscle relaxation

Question 273 of 304 : Physiology
What is the major stimulus for secretion of enzyme-rich fluid by pancreatic acinar cells? ... Cholecystokinin

Question 274 of 304 : Physiology
What is the major stimulus for zymogen release, but a poor stimulus for bicarbonate secretion? ... Acetylcholine

Question 275 of 304 : Physiology
What is the only types of carbohydrate that is absorbed? ... Monosacharides

Question 276 of 304 : Physiology
What is the primary location over bacterial conversion of conjugated bilirubin to urobilinogen? ... Colon

Question 277 of 304 : Physiology
What is Zollinger-Ellison syndrome? What is the main manifestation? ... 1. Hypersecretion of Gastrin 2. Peptic ulcers

Question 278 of 304 : Physiology
What manifestations are seen in pancreatic insufficiency? ... -malabsorption -steatorrhea (greasy, malodorous stool)

Question 279 of 304 : Physiology
What regulates bicarbonate secretion? ... Stimulated by secretin, potentiated by vagal input and CCK

Question 280 of 304 : Physiology
What regulates CCK secretion? ... Stimulated by fatty acids and amino acids

Question 281 of 304 : Physiology
What regulates Gastrin secretion? ... -Stimulated by stomach distension, amino acids, peptides, and vagus -Inhibited by secretin and stomach acid pH less than 1.5

Question 282 of 304 : Physiology
What regulates secretion of secretin? ... Stimulated by acid and fatty acids in lumen of duodenum

Question 283 of 304 : Physiology
What regulates secretion of Somatostatin? ... -Stimulated by acid -Inhibited by vagus

Question 284 of 304 : Physiology
What special characteristic do bile salts possess? ... They are amphipathic (contain both hydrophilic and hydrophobic domains)

Question 285 of 304 : Physiology
What special characteristic does the conjugated form of bilirubin possess? ... It is water soluble.

Question 286 of 304 : Physiology
What substance stimulates ductal cells to secrete bicarbonate-rich fluid? ... Secretin

Question 287 of 304 : Physiology
What three enzymes aid in fat digestion? ... 1. Lipase 2. Phospholipase A 3. Colipase

Question 288 of 304 : Physiology
What trasport is utilized in glucose absorption across cell membrane? ... Sodium-glucose-coupled transporter

Question 289 of 304 : Physiology
What two conditions are caused be autoimmune destruction of parietal cells? ... -Chronic Gastritis -Pernicious Anemia

Question 290 of 304 : Physiology
What type(s) of innervation stimulate salivary secretion? ... BOTH Sympathetic and Parasympathetic

Question 291 of 304 : Physiology
Where are the oligosaccharide hydrolase enzymes located? ... At the brush border of the intestine

Question 292 of 304 : Physiology
Where does bilirubin conjugation take place? ... Liver

Question 293 of 304 : Physiology
Where does glucose absorption occur? ... Duodenum and proximal Jejunum

Question 294 of 304 : Physiology
Where does heme catabolism take place? ... In the Reticuloendothelial System

Question 295 of 304 : Physiology
Where is bicarbonate secreted and what does it do? ... -Surface mucosal cells of stomach and duodenum -Neutralizes acid -Present in the unstirred layer preventing autodigestion

Question 296 of 304 : Physiology
Where is Cholecystokinin (CCK) secreted? ... I cells of duodenum and jejunum

Question 297 of 304 : Physiology
Where is Secretin secreted? ... S cells of duodenum

Question 298 of 304 : Physiology
Where is Somatostatin secreted? ... D cells in pancreatic islets and GI mucosa

Question 299 of 304 : Physiology
Where is Vasoactive Intestinal Peptide (VIP) secreted? ... Smooth muscle nerves of the intestines

Question 300 of 304 : Physiology
Which component of bile makes up the greatest percentage? ... Water (97%)

Question 301 of 304 : Physiology
Which component of bile solubilizes lipids in micelles for absorption? ... Bile salts

Question 302 of 304 : Physiology
Which component of saliva begins starch digestion? ... Alpha-amylase (ptyalin)

Question 303 of 304 : Physiology
Which component of saliva lubricates food? ... Mucins (glycoproteins)

Question 304 of 304 : Physiology
Why do we need alkaline pancreatic juice in the duodenum? ... To neutralize gastric acid, allowing pancreatic enzymes to function
